text: All plants absorb Na+ from their environment when grown under saline conditions. Once it enters the symplast, Na+ is distributed throughout various cells, tissues, and organs. While it can serve as a beneficial osmoticum to lower cellular water potential, it also carries the risk of severe toxicity. Precise control over Na+ fluxes is therefore vital for survival. Current research indicates that the distinction between salt-tolerant and salt-sensitive species does not stem from different types of transporters, but rather from the sophisticated regulation of Na+ uptake and internal movement. Recently, several regulatory mechanisms have been identified, involving signaling molecules such as Ca2+, cyclic nucleotides, reactive oxygen species, and hormones, as well as complex transcriptional and post-translational modifications. This review provides a comprehensive overview of intra- and intercellular Na+ transport and summarizes current understanding of how these pathways are modulated during the critical early stages of salt stress.
